questions.reviewPearl: Adjustment disorder features symptoms that are out of proportion to the stressor's severity and cause significant functional impairment, but do not meet the criteria for another specific mental disorder.\
questions.stem: A 20-year-old college student comes to student health services because she has been feeling "terrible" for the past week. She has been tearful and distracted since her boyfriend of 5 months broke up with her 1 week ago. She feels that she has a lot to offer in a relationship and is sad that things did not work out. She performed poorly on a test 3 days ago and has not been sleeping well. She asks if she should get something to help her sleep. She usually drinks four beers weekly, but she drank three beers 2 nights ago. She appears tired but well nourished and well groomed. Her pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is tearful when discussing her relationship but becomes bright and engaged when she talks about her career plans and interest in art history. She thinks she is a good person and that hopefully things will get better. Her hematocrit is 34%, and mean corpuscular volume is 79 m³. Urine toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate adjustment disorder from normal sadness and major depressive disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient’s emotional distress and functional impairment (poor test score) occurred in direct response to an identifiable psychosocial stressor (breakup) within the past 3 months.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: She does not meet the criteria for Major Depressive Disorder (lacks 5 of 9 SIGECAPS symptoms) or Acute Stress Disorder (requires life-threatening trauma).

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Adjustment disorder features symptoms that are out of proportion to the stressor's severity and cause significant functional impairment, but do not meet the criteria for another specific mental disorder.\

questions.reviewPearl: Conduct disorder is a common childhood precursor to antisocial personality disorder in adulthood.\
questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy is brought by his parents for a well-child examination. His parents report that he is rambunctious with a high energy level, and he has been retained in one grade. He sleeps approximately 6½ hours nightly. He has always been a handful and difficult to discipline; he argues about everything. For the past 2 years, he has often refused to go to school, so that his father then has to drive him there and walk him into his classroom. Several times, he has left school during the day. One month ago, he killed the family cat when he was playing too roughly. Although he said that it was an accident, he is not remorseful. His older brother is currently in a juvenile detention facility for grand larceny. The patient is at the 50th percentile for height and weight. His vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows several ecchymoses in various stages of healing over the extremities. During the examination, he picks his nose. On mental status examination, he is initially insolent, but then he describes how he worries at night that something will happen to someone in his family. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose conduct disorder based on behavior that violates the basic rights of others or major societal norms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Cruelty to animals, lack of remorse, and chronic rule violations (such as persistent truancy) in a patient under age 15 are diagnostic for conduct disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Antisocial personality disorder cannot be diagnosed before age 18; ADHD may present with impulsivity but does not account for severe, intentional rule-breaking or animal cruelty.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Conduct disorder is a common childhood precursor to antisocial personality disorder in adulthood.\

questions.reviewPearl: Extreme low body weight in anorexia nervosa suppresses GnRH release, leading to decreased FSH/LH and subsequent functional hypothalamic amenorrhea.\
questions.stem: A 16-year-old high school student will not eat meals with her family, preferring to eat alone in the kitchen. Her mother reports that she found food hidden under her daughter's bed. The patient does well in school, but her mother is concerned because her daughter does not date. The patient is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 44 kg (96 lb). She is most likely to have a history of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify physiological complications secondary to anorexia nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has a BMI of roughly 15.6 kg/m² and behaviors consistent with anorexia nervosa, which profoundly suppresses the HPO axis leading to secondary amenorrhea.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Dental caries and diarrhea are more strongly associated with the purging or laxative-abuse subtypes of bulimia nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Extreme low body weight in anorexia nervosa suppresses GnRH release, leading to decreased FSH/LH and subsequent functional hypothalamic amenorrhea.\

questions.reviewPearl: Patients with Lewy body dementia are extremely sensitive to the extrapyramidal side effects of typical and atypical antipsychotics.\
questions.stem: A 77-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of a 6-month history of confusion and "slowing down." She says he talks to people who are not there. The patient says he sometimes sees and has conversations with nonthreatening persons sitting by his bedroom window. He is otherwise asymptomatic. He has hypertension. Fifteen years ago, he underwent coronary artery bypass grafting for coronary artery disease. Current medications are atenolol and 81-mg aspirin. He retired from his position as a pharmacist 6 years ago. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 102/min and irregular, and blood pressure is 145/100 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Muscle tone in all the extremities is decreased. Gait is slow and narrow based. On mental status examination, his affect is pleasant but dulled. He can recall one of three words after 2 minutes. He reports serial sevens as 100, 91, 73, and 65. He is unable to spell "world" backward or draw the hands on a clock accurately. Results of a complete blood count and serum studies are within the reference ranges. An ECG shows a regular sinus rhythm. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the clinical presentation of Lewy body dementia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient presents with the classic triad for Lewy body dementia: progressive cognitive decline, well-formed visual hallucinations, and parkinsonian motor features (slow gait).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Alzheimer disease typically lacks early prominent visual hallucinations and motor parkinsonism; vascular dementia classically presents with a stepwise cognitive decline.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Patients with Lewy body dementia are extremely sensitive to the extrapyramidal side effects of typical and atypical antipsychotics.\

questions.reviewPearl: Neuromuscular excitability (hyperreflexia, myoclonus) helps distinguish Serotonin Syndrome from Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome clinically.\
questions.stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of increasing confusion and agitation since he came home from work 2 hours ago. He first said he had a headache and then began pacing around the house, saying that he felt anxious. One week ago, he strained his back and began taking tramadol. He has a 4-year history of major depressive disorder treated with fluoxetine. He is anxious and diaphoretic. He is oriented to person but not to place and time. His temperature is 40°C (104°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 155/95 mm Hg. Physical examination shows dilated pupils, hyperreflexia, and generalized muscle rigidity. Dysregulation of which of the following neurotransmitters is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify serotonin syndrome etiology and physiological presentation.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The combination of fluoxetine (an SSRI) and tramadol (which has serotonergic activity) triggered serotonin syndrome, characterized by autonomic instability, fever, and hyperreflexia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Dysregulation of dopamine is the underlying cause of Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome, which presents with lead-pipe rigidity but normal or decreased reflexes, not hyperreflexia.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Neuromuscular excitability (hyperreflexia, myoclonus) helps distinguish Serotonin Syndrome from Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome clinically.\

questions.reviewPearl: Psychotherapy is the treatment of choice for adjustment disorders, primarily focusing on developing coping mechanisms, problem-solving, and emotional resilience.\
questions.stem: A 42-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. He has had fatigue, anxiety, and decreased work performance since his divorce 2 months ago. He has not gained or lost weight and has not been depressed. He takes no medications, and he does not smoke or use drugs; he drinks one or two alcoholic beverages weekly. There is no family history of psychiatric illness. He appears anxious, but his affect is otherwise normal. Physical examination is noncontributory. Complete blood count, biochemical profile, and thyroid function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Manage adjustment disorder with appropriate non-pharmacological therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing an adjustment disorder with anxiety triggered by his recent divorce, and supportive psychotherapy is the indicated first-line treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Pharmacological interventions such as SSRIs (fluoxetine) or sleep aids are not indicated as first-line therapy for an uncomplicated adjustment disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Psychotherapy is the treatment of choice for adjustment disorders, primarily focusing on developing coping mechanisms, problem-solving, and emotional resilience.\

questions.reviewPearl: Serotonin deficiency in the central nervous system is a primary biological marker for impulsive aggression and a high risk of completed suicide.\
questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital after a suicide attempt by an overdose of pills and alcohol. She has a 6-year history of major depressive disorder treated with various antidepressants. During this period, she has attempted suicide by hanging herself twice, jumping from a bridge, and taking several overdoses of pills. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m². Her pulse is 64/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Mental status examination shows a depressed mood. She says that she has had difficulty sleeping and that she feels hopeless and helpless. There is still evidence of suicidal ideation. Laboratory findings are within the reference range. Urine toxicology screening is negative. This patient is most likely to have which of the following abnormalities?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Decreased concentration of 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid in cerebrospinal fluid analysis

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Delayed REM sleep on nighttime polysomnography

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Enlarged lateral ventricles on CT scan of the head

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Serology positive for human leukocyte antigen-DR2

questions.educationalObjective: Associate CSF 5-HIAA levels with suicidal and impulsive behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Low levels of CSF 5-HIAA (the primary metabolite of serotonin) strongly correlate with highly lethal suicidal behavior and impulsivity.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Depression usually features shortened, not delayed, REM sleep latency. Enlarged ventricles are classically seen in schizophrenia, and lactate infusion sensitivity is tied to panic disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Serotonin deficiency in the central nervous system is a primary biological marker for impulsive aggression and a high risk of completed suicide.\

questions.reviewPearl: Parent management training (PMT) is the most robustly supported first-line intervention for young children exhibiting disruptive behavior disorders.\
questions.stem: A healthy 4-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her father because of behavior problems during the past 6 months. He reports that his daughter has been having increasingly frequent temper tantrums and has shown increased disobedience and active and angry defiance. Last night, she threw her dinner plate across the table at her younger brother. He notes that her behavior is appropriate at day care and when she visits her grandmother. She has not been hyperactive. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. During the examination, the patient is generally compliant with the physician's directions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassure the father that this is normal behavior

questions.educationalObjective: Manage early-onset oppositional defiant behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Oppositional defiant behavior that is isolated primarily to the home environment is optimally addressed first with parent management training.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Reassurance is incorrect as throwing objects at siblings crosses the boundaries of normal toddler behavior. Medications are not indicated for early childhood oppositional defiance.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Parent management training (PMT) is the most robustly supported first-line intervention for young children exhibiting disruptive behavior disorders.\

questions.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines are the safest and most effective initial psychopharmacologic agents for sympathomimetic and stimulant toxicities.\
questions.stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his roommate because of a 5-hour history of confusion and agitation. The patient says that bugs are crawling all over his skin. He has been using cocaine six times daily for the past 3 days. On arrival, he is hypervigilant and agitated. He is oriented to person, place, and time. His temperature is 38.3°C (101°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 32/min, and blood pressure is 150/105 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Urine toxicology screening is positive for benzoylecgonine. An ECG shows a prolonged QT interval. In addition to intravenous hydration and supportive measures, which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Treat acute cocaine intoxication and resulting agitation safely.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines, such as clonazepam, are the first-line treatment for cocaine toxicity to reduce agitation, dampen sympathetic drive, and lower seizure risk.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Antipsychotics like ziprasidone should be avoided in this scenario because they can further prolong the QT interval and lower the seizure threshold.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Benzodiazepines are the safest and most effective initial psychopharmacologic agents for sympathomimetic and stimulant toxicities.\

questions.reviewPearl:
Obsessions in OCD are typically ego-dystonic, meaning the patient finds them distressing and out of character, strongly differentiating them from delusions.\
Here are the extracted questions, answer choices, and generated explanations for Questions 11 through 20 based on the source material.\
questions.stem: A 32-year-old man comes to the physician because he thinks he might be losing his mind. For the past year, he has been having intrusive thoughts of killing his 4-year-old son. He does not want to do this and considers the thoughts ridiculous. He is becoming increasingly depressed and is worried that something is happening to him. He is a pastor at a local church and says that the thoughts are especially prevalent when he is reading scriptures and preparing for a sermon. He has not told anyone, including his wife, about "these repulsive ideas." He says that he is a good father and takes good care of his son. During his first year of college, he was treated briefly with counseling for an adjustment problem. He says that he does not think he has a family history of psychiatric illness, but his mother is considered to be overly anxious. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows an embarrassed, mildly anxious affect. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose obsessive-compulsive disorder based on the presentation of ego-dystonic obsessions.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient’s unwanted, intrusive, and highly distressing violent thoughts are classic ego-dystonic obsessions characteristic of obsessive-compulsive disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: The patient is not experiencing a psychotic disorder (like schizophrenia) because he retains full insight, recognizing that the thoughts are "ridiculous" and unwanted.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Obsessions in OCD are typically ego-dystonic, meaning the patient finds them distressing and out of character, strongly differentiating them from delusions.\
Here are the extracted questions, answer choices, and generated explanations for Questions 11 through 20 based on the source material.\

questions.reviewPearl: Enuresis is not considered a pathological diagnosis until a child is at least 5 years old.\
questions.stem: A 3½-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents because he has continued to wet his bed approximately two times weekly since being daytime toilet trained at the age of 2 years. They report that their two daughters were toilet trained by the time they were 2 years of age and have never wet their beds since that time. They have tried all of the available treatments including a bed-wetting alarm system and waking the child up each night at midnight to have him go to the bathroom. They believe that he is wetting the bed just to make them angry. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassurance that this is normal behavior

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize normal childhood development regarding nighttime continence.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Bedwetting (enuresis) in a 3½-year-old is developmentally normal.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Pharmacotherapy (like imipramine) or conditioning alarms are not indicated until the child reaches a developmental age of at least 5 years.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Enuresis is not considered a pathological diagnosis until a child is at least 5 years old.\

questions.reviewPearl: The primary distinction between bulimia nervosa and the binge-eating/purging subtype of anorexia nervosa is absolute body weight.\
questions.stem: A 17-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because her daughter has lost 4 kg (9 lb) during the past month and has seemed increasingly irritable and fatigued. Her mother reports that during the past 2 weeks her daughter also has been drinking more water and going to the bathroom more frequently. The patient's last menstrual period was 4 months ago. Menarche was at the age of 14 years, and her menses previously occurred at regular 28-day intervals. She says that she often feels cold and has occasional constipation and abdominal pain. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She appears malnourished. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 41 kg (90 lb); BMI is 16 kg/m². Her temperature is 35.8°C (96.5°F), pulse is 55/min, respirations are 8/min, and blood pressure is 90/55 mm Hg. The skin is dry. Abdominal examination shows no guarding or rebound. On mental status examination, she has a depressed mood and an irritable affect. She says it upsets her when her mother suggests that she does not eat enough. She worries about being overweight and reports that she recently has been bingeing on large amounts of food and then purging through self-induced vomiting. Laboratory studies show: [Hb 12, Hct 35, MCV 90, WBC 4000, Na 140, K 3.4, Cl 93, HCO3 32, Mg 1.4, Chol 250, TSH 3.9, T4 6, T3 95]. An ECG shows sinus bradycardia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate anorexia nervosa from bulimia nervosa based on patient BMI.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has a profoundly low BMI (16 kg/m²), intense fear of weight gain, and secondary amenorrhea, diagnosing the binge-eating/purging subtype of anorexia nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Bulimia nervosa also involves bingeing and purging but requires the patient to have a normal or elevated body weight.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: The primary distinction between bulimia nervosa and the binge-eating/purging subtype of anorexia nervosa is absolute body weight.\

questions.retrievalTag: Normal sleep changes elderly phase advance
questions.reviewPearl: Normal aging is associated with a circadian phase advance, decreased REM sleep, and reduced slow-wave (deep) sleep.\
questions.stem: An 82-year-old man comes to the physician with his wife because of difficulty sleeping during the past year. He goes to bed around 10 pm and is able to fall asleep easily but awakens around 4 am and is unable to return to sleep. His wife says she has not noticed any changes in his sleeping habits, such as restlessness, snoring, or any other irregularity in breathing. He sometimes dozes off when he is reading a newspaper during the day or watching television at night. He has allergic rhinitis treated with loratadine as needed. His only other medication is a daily multivitamin. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is alert. He has a euthymic mood and reactive affect. He is pleasant and cooperative but appears anxious when describing his problem sleeping. His Mini-Mental State Examination score is 28/30. He says the day is Tuesday, but it is Wednesday, and he makes one error when performing serial sevens. In addition to providing instructions about sleep hygiene, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize normal age-related sleep architecture changes in the elderly.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's "early to bed, early to rise" pattern and fragmented sleep are normal age-related changes in circadian rhythm (advanced sleep phase).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: There are no signs of sleep apnea (no snoring), so CPAP or pulse oximetry are unnecessary; modafinil is not indicated for normal aging.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Normal aging is associated with a circadian phase advance, decreased REM sleep, and reduced slow-wave (deep) sleep.\

questions.reviewPearl: Amphetamine intoxication can mimic acute paranoid schizophrenia but is frequently accompanied by sympathetic hyperactivity and hyperreflexia.\
questions.stem: The response options for the next 2 items are the same. Select one answer for each item in the set. For each patient with hallucinations, select the most likely diagnosis. A 27-year-old man comes to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision. He states that he feels that he is being followed by the FBI. His temperature is 37.6°C (99.7°F), pulse is 96/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 130/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows mild hyperreflexia; there is no evidence of head trauma, and no other abnormalities are noted. On mental status examination, he is agitated with affective lability and rapid speech. He is oriented only to person and place; other tests of the mental status examination cannot be successfully completed.

answerChoices.label: G
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: Identify the clinical presentation of amphetamine intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Paranoia, agitation, affective lability, rapid speech, and hyperreflexia without other organic causes point strongly to amphetamine (stimulant) intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Hallucinogens predominantly cause perceptual distortions (visual hallucinations) rather than isolated paranoia, and opioid intoxication causes CNS depression.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Amphetamine intoxication can mimic acute paranoid schizophrenia but is frequently accompanied by sympathetic hyperactivity and hyperreflexia.\

questions.reviewPearl: Classical hallucinogens cause profound visual hallucinations and mydriasis, typically without the severe tachycardia or hypertension seen in stimulant toxicity.\
questions.stem: For each patient with hallucinations, select the most likely diagnosis. A previously healthy 25-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her girlfriend because of a sudden behavior change. She is anxious because "everyone has turned into spacemen." She avoids the medical staff, stating that they look like "large furry monsters." She sees "multicolored snakes singing Hail Mary" on the floor. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 76/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 118/66 mm Hg. Mucous membranes are dry. Cranial nerves are intact. Neurologic examination shows normal findings except for mydriasis. On mental status examination, she is agitated, frightened, and fully oriented to person, place, and time. Toxicology screening is negative.

answerChoices.label: G
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose hallucinogen intoxication based on characteristic perceptual alterations.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Elaborate visual hallucinations, perceptual distortions, intact orientation, and mydriasis with otherwise stable vital signs are classic for hallucinogen (e.g., LSD) intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Routine urine toxicology screens typically do not test for classical hallucinogens. Amphetamines would cause significant vital sign derangements.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Classical hallucinogens cause profound visual hallucinations and mydriasis, typically without the severe tachycardia or hypertension seen in stimulant toxicity.\

questions.reviewPearl: Bupropion is a preferred first-line antidepressant for patients concerned about weight gain, sexual dysfunction, or pronounced fatigue.\
questions.stem: A 67-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of increased fatigue and decreased libido. He used to sleep 8 hours every night, but now he sleeps 10 hours every night and takes a nap during the day. He has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight gain during this period. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus; hypertension; degenerative arthritis of the back, hips, and knees; and hypercholesterolemia. Current medications include metformin, ibuprofen, simvastatin, sildenafil, lisinopril, and hydrochlorothiazide. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 104 kg (230 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m². His pulse is 74/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Pedal pulses are decreased. Examination shows Heberden nodes over the distal interphalangeal joints. Sensation to pinprick is decreased over the feet. Mental status examination shows a constricted affect. He says he is losing interest in life because his multiple medical problems are so difficult to manage. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. Serum studies show: [Glucose 155, Chol 162, HDL 46, LDL 90, Triglycerides 128]. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Add bupropion to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Add insulin to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Add venlafaxine to the medication regimen

questions.educationalObjective: Select an appropriate antidepressant based on side-effect profiles and patient comorbidities.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has Major Depressive Disorder with hypersomnia, weight gain, and sexual dysfunction. Bupropion is an activating antidepressant that does not cause weight gain or sexual side effects.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Adding venlafaxine (an SNRI) or an SSRI would likely exacerbate his existing sexual dysfunction.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Bupropion is a preferred first-line antidepressant for patients concerned about weight gain, sexual dysfunction, or pronounced fatigue.\

questions.retrievalTag: Circadian rhythm sleep disorder delayed sleep phase
questions.reviewPearl: Patients with delayed sleep phase disorder have a shifted circadian pacemaker but normal sleep architecture when allowed to sleep on their delayed schedule.\
questions.stem: A 27-year-old teacher comes to the physician requesting a prescription for sleep medication. She has been unable to fall asleep until 2 or 3 am since the start of the school year 3 months ago. When she awakens at 6 am, she feels drowsy and remains tired throughout the morning. Last week, she fell asleep at her desk while her students were watching a movie. She believes that her job is in jeopardy. Drinking one glass of wine before going to bed has not helped her fall asleep. She recently took one of her mother's zolpidem tablets; she only slept for 2 hours and was then awake until 3 or 4 am. During vacations and on weekends, she typically sleeps from 3 am until noon and feels energetic and well rested. She currently does not take any medications or drink alcohol. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she appears tired and is teary. She can recall two of three objects after 5 minutes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose circadian rhythm sleep disorder, delayed sleep phase type.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The inability to sleep at conventional times, combined with normal, restorative sleep when allowed to follow her internal clock (3 am to noon), defines the delayed sleep phase type of circadian rhythm sleep disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Primary insomnia features difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep regardless of the allowed schedule, and would not resolve on weekends.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Patients with delayed sleep phase disorder have a shifted circadian pacemaker but normal sleep architecture when allowed to sleep on their delayed schedule.\

questions.reviewPearl: Restless legs syndrome features an irresistible urge to move the legs accompanied by dysesthesias, worsening at night/rest, and temporarily relieved by movement.\
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because of a 6-month history of difficulty sleeping and fatigue. She describes an uncomfortable sensation in her calves that she first notices a few minutes after going to bed; the sensation can keep her awake for up to 2 hours. Although she occasionally snores enough to awaken her husband, once asleep, she sleeps soundly. She has had migraines since she was a teenager and currently takes sumatriptan approximately once monthly. Menopause occurred 6 years ago. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m². Her pulse is 70/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a small patch of eczema over the right antecubital fossa. Mental status examination shows mild anxiety. She is concerned that her fatigue is affecting her work performance. Her husband recently began to consider retirement, and she knows how much he is counting on her salary. She gets discouraged when she cannot sleep and recalls crying one night when she became particularly frustrated. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the clinical presentation of restless legs syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The uncomfortable sensation in her calves occurring at rest (going to bed) that delays sleep onset is classic for restless legs syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Obstructive sleep apnea causes excessive daytime sleepiness and frequent nocturnal awakenings, not sleep-onset difficulty due to leg sensations.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Restless legs syndrome features an irresistible urge to move the legs accompanied by dysesthesias, worsening at night/rest, and temporarily relieved by movement.\

questions.reviewPearl: Lateral ventricular enlargement in schizophrenia results from progressive loss of cortical tissue volume and may be present even at the first episode of psychosis.\
questions.stem: A 47-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 1 hour after he inadvertently struck his head on a glass door that he mistakenly believed was open. He fell to the ground for a few seconds and said that he felt faint. His parents had to assist him to a chair. He did not lose consciousness and recalls the entire sequence of events. He was diagnosed with schizophrenia at the age of 17 years and was institutionalized until the age of 19 years. He has lived at home with his parents since that time. His only medication is olanzapine. His pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg while supine; while standing, his pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 105/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a 2-cm circular ecchymosis on the left side of the forehead. Neurologic examination shows a fine tremor and occasional protrusions of the tongue. He is oriented to person, place, and time. A CT scan of the head is most likely to show which of the following?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Accumulation of fluid in the subdural space in the left temporoparietal area

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Bilateral calcification of the nuclei basales

questions.educationalObjective: Identify common structural neuroimaging findings associated with schizophrenia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has a long-standing history of schizophrenia; symmetrical enlargement of the lateral ventricles is the most widely replicated structural brain abnormality in this disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Subdural hematomas or brain contusions are highly unlikely given the minor mechanism of injury, lack of focal deficits, and no loss of consciousness.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Lateral ventricular enlargement in schizophrenia results from progressive loss of cortical tissue volume and may be present even at the first episode of psychosis.\

questions.reviewPearl:
The pathophysiology of Tourette syndrome involves dopaminergic hyperactivity in the striatum, which is effectively managed by dopamine D2 receptor antagonists like haloperidol.\
Here are the extracted questions, answer choices, and explanations for Questions 21 through 30 based on the provided self-assessment and answer key.\
questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because of a 2-week history of disruptive behavior in school. His teacher reports that he has been screaming out during class for no apparent reason. He has a 4-year history of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. He takes no medications. Last year, he was seen by an allergist because of sniffing, clearing his throat, and rubbing his nose repeatedly. His symptoms resolved spontaneously within 1 month. Examination today shows intermittent, stereotyped, purposeless movements, including grimacing, raising his eyebrows, and shrugging his left shoulder. After 2 days of treatment with haloperidol, his symptoms improve. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's response to pharmacotherapy?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Decreased binding of acetylcholine at the postsynaptic receptor

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Decreased release of acetylcholine from the presynaptic terminal

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Decreased binding of dopamine at the postsynaptic receptor

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Decreased release of dopamine from the presynaptic terminal

answerChoices.label: G
answerChoices.text: Increased binding of acetylcholine at the postsynaptic receptor

answerChoices.label: I
answerChoices.text: Increased release of acetylcholine from the presynaptic terminal

answerChoices.label: J
answerChoices.text: Increased binding of dopamine at the postsynaptic receptor

answerChoices.label: L
answerChoices.text: Increased release of dopamine from the presynaptic terminal

questions.educationalObjective: Understand the mechanism of action of typical antipsychotics in the management of Tourette syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has Tourette syndrome (multiple motor tics and at least one vocal tic). Haloperidol treats tics via potent D2 dopamine receptor antagonism, decreasing dopamine binding at the postsynaptic receptor.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Haloperidol does not primarily work by affecting presynaptic dopamine release or altering acetylcholine dynamics.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
The pathophysiology of Tourette syndrome involves dopaminergic hyperactivity in the striatum, which is effectively managed by dopamine D2 receptor antagonists like haloperidol.\
Here are the extracted questions, answer choices, and explanations for Questions 21 through 30 based on the provided self-assessment and answer key.\

questions.reviewPearl: Pinpoint pupils in a comatose patient with respiratory depression is highly specific for opioid overdose and should prompt immediate administration of naloxone.\
questions.stem: A 46-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department in respiratory depression 30 minutes after attempting suicide by ingesting a large amount of an unknown drug. On arrival, she is intubated and mechanically ventilated. She is unconscious but responds to sternal rub by moaning. Examination shows pinpoint pupils. Diffuse crackles are heard on auscultation. Which of the following is the most likely drug ingested?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the classic clinical triad of opioid intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient presents with the classic triad of opioid overdose: profound respiratory depression, altered mental status (coma), and pinpoint pupils (miosis).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Barbiturates and benzodiazepines cause central nervous system and respiratory depression, but do not classically present with pinpoint pupils.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Pinpoint pupils in a comatose patient with respiratory depression is highly specific for opioid overdose and should prompt immediate administration of naloxone.\

questions.reviewPearl: Active homicidal ideation, especially in the context of command hallucinations, is an absolute indication for inpatient psychiatric hospitalization to ensure safety.\
questions.stem: A 21-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by police after threatening her boyfriend with a meat cleaver. She picked up the cleaver after he pulled her by the hair when she did not bring him a drink. She says that she cannot stand the way he orders her around. Two years ago, she was diagnosed with major depressive disorder and successfully treated with an antidepressant. Her boyfriend flushed her pills down the toilet 2 months ago, and she never refilled her prescription. She reports now feeling the same way she did prior to starting the medication. She was fired from her job 2 weeks ago when her company declared bankruptcy. She has no family in the area. Physical examination shows multiple ecchymoses over the extremities in various stages of healing. Pelvic examination shows multiple vaginal abrasions. On mental status examination, she is tearful and has a labile affect. She avoids eye contact and repeatedly says, "I am so weak." When asked about suicidal ideation, she states that she would kill her boyfriend rather than kill herself. She denies delusions but says she sometimes hears her dead mother's voice telling her to defend herself. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Encourage the patient to press charges against her boyfriend

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Contact the patient's boyfriend to schedule couples counseling

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Admit the patient to the psychiatric unit

questions.educationalObjective: Identify indications for involuntary or urgent inpatient psychiatric admission based on imminent homicidal risk.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient expresses active homicidal ideation with a specific target and means, alongside experiencing command hallucinations, making her an imminent threat to others. This warrants immediate psychiatric admission.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Reinitiating outpatient therapy or groups does not ensure immediate safety. Couples counseling is contraindicated in cases of active domestic violence.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Active homicidal ideation, especially in the context of command hallucinations, is an absolute indication for inpatient psychiatric hospitalization to ensure safety.\

questions.reviewPearl: The FDA black box warning requires close clinical observation (e.g., weekly) when initiating antidepressants in patients under age 25 to monitor for new or worsening suicidality.\
questions.stem: A 24-year-old male college student comes to student health services for a follow-up examination 2 weeks after starting sertraline for treatment of major depressive disorder. Before starting the medication, the patient had a 6-month history of decreased motivation, lack of interest in academic work and fraternity activities, and feelings of sadness and worthlessness. He also had a decreased appetite and difficulty falling asleep at night. He had thought about suicide but made no attempts. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he says that he feels better but still has difficulty sleeping. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Maintain the current dosage of sertraline and schedule a follow-up examination in 4 months

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Maintain the current dosage of sertraline and schedule weekly follow-up examinations for the next month

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Maintain the current dosage of sertraline and add amitriptyline to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Increase the dosage of sertraline and schedule a follow-up examination in 2 months

questions.educationalObjective: Manage the follow-up schedule for young adults starting SSRI therapy safely.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Antidepressants take 4-6 weeks to reach full efficacy. The patient is showing early improvement at 2 weeks, so maintaining the dose is appropriate. However, patients under 25 are at an increased risk of worsening suicidal ideation upon initiation of SSRIs and require close, frequent monitoring.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Adjusting the medication dose or switching drugs is premature at 2 weeks. Follow-ups at 2 or 4 months are too infrequent for a 24-year-old with a history of suicidality recently started on an SSRI.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: The FDA black box warning requires close clinical observation (e.g., weekly) when initiating antidepressants in patients under age 25 to monitor for new or worsening suicidality.\

questions.retrievalTag: Acute intermittent porphyria psychiatric abdominal pain
questions.reviewPearl: Think of acute intermittent porphyria in patients presenting with new-onset psychiatric symptoms accompanied by unexplained acute abdominal pain and neuropathic signs.\
questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of suicidal thoughts, depression, emotional lability, and poor concentration. She is unable to recall when her symptoms began. Her father and sister have a history of similar symptoms. She also has had intermittent abdominal pain. She does not drink alcohol or take medications because they exacerbate her gastrointestinal distress. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. Neurologic examination shows decreased joint position sense. Romberg sign is present. Mental status examination shows a depressed mood. There is evidence of suicidal ideation. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify the clinical triad of acute intermittent porphyria.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's triad of unexplained acute abdominal pain, neuropsychiatric symptoms (depression, suicidality), and peripheral neuropathy (decreased joint position sense, positive Romberg), combined with a family history and medication sensitivity, classically point to acute intermittent porphyria.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Somatization disorder cannot explain objective neurological deficits like a positive Romberg sign. Multiple sclerosis typically lacks severe, unexplained abdominal pain.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Think of acute intermittent porphyria in patients presenting with new-onset psychiatric symptoms accompanied by unexplained acute abdominal pain and neuropathic signs.\

questions.reviewPearl: Congenital heart defects, particularly endocardial cushion defects and ventricular septal defects, occur in approximately 50% of children with Down syndrome.\
questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents because of difficulty learning. They are concerned that she is not "catching on" as quickly as the other children in her kindergarten class. She is confused even by simple homework assignments and struggles to write the alphabet. Her teacher describes her as a happy, eager-to-please child who is having trouble learning. She appears to enjoy going to school and carries her backpack with pride. She wears glasses. She is at the 30th percentile for height and 65th percentile for weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows epicanthal folds. A grade 2/6 murmur is heard best at the left sternal border. She can run well but climbs stairs by placing both feet on each step before proceeding to the next one. She has difficulty hopping on one foot. She can recite the alphabet and identify half of the letters. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the physical and developmental presentation of Down syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The presentation of learning difficulties, delayed gross motor milestones, epicanthal folds, and a cardiac murmur (commonly VSD or AVSD) is highly characteristic of Down syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Fetal alcohol syndrome presents with a smooth philtrum and thin vermillion border, not typically epicanthal folds. Fragile X syndrome generally presents with long faces and prominent ears, more prominently in males.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Congenital heart defects, particularly endocardial cushion defects and ventricular septal defects, occur in approximately 50% of children with Down syndrome.\

questions.reviewPearl: Panic attacks frequently mimic acute coronary syndromes but typically peak rapidly and resolve quickly, presenting with intense apprehension alongside autonomic hyperactivity.\
questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of daily episodes of dizziness and feeling as though she were about to pass out. The episodes last for less than 5 minutes and are accompanied by a flushing sensation throughout her body. She says she is afraid to go to sleep because she fears that her heart will stop and she will never wake up. She suspects that her symptoms are caused by stress and may indicate recurrent heart problems. During the past 3 months, she has gone to the emergency department four times for treatment of chest pain. Evaluation each time showed no abnormalities, and she was told that she was taking too many nitroglycerin capsules without benefit. She had a myocardial infarction 6 years ago and underwent stent placement. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 105/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 145/88 mm Hg. There is no venous distention. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she appears anxious and says that her mood has been down. She reports that any twinge in her chest makes her worry that she is going to die. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish panic disorder from somatic symptom or acute medical emergencies in patients with prior health conditions.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Recurrent, brief (< 5 min) episodes of severe autonomic arousal, an intense fear of dying, and recurrent negative emergency department workups for cardiac events describe panic disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While she has a history of CAD, the negative ED workups rule out acute myocardial infarction as the cause of her current acute, brief, highly anxious episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Panic attacks frequently mimic acute coronary syndromes but typically peak rapidly and resolve quickly, presenting with intense apprehension alongside autonomic hyperactivity.\

questions.retrievalTag: Tardive dyskinesia risk haloperidol to aripiprazole
questions.reviewPearl: Second-generation antipsychotics present a considerably lower risk of extrapyramidal symptoms and tardive dyskinesia because of their weaker D2 receptor antagonism compared to high-potency typicals.\
questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman with schizophrenia, paranoid type, comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. She has been treated with haloperidol for 15 years, and her symptoms have been well controlled. She has no history of serious medical illness. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has a neutral mood and a blunted affect. She reports no auditory or visual hallucinations. She says she knows a number of patients with schizophrenia who have tardive dyskinesia and says that she would like to decrease her own risk for this problem. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Add citalopram to the current regimen

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Add lorazepam to the current regimen

questions.educationalObjective: Manage the risk of tardive dyskinesia by transitioning from first- to second-generation antipsychotics.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic associated with a high risk of developing tardive dyskinesia (TD). Switching to an atypical (second-generation) antipsychotic like aripiprazole significantly lowers the risk of developing this irreversible side effect.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Decreasing the dose of haloperidol may not sufficiently lower her TD risk and might precipitate psychosis. Maintaining the current regimen ignores the valid concern for TD.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Second-generation antipsychotics present a considerably lower risk of extrapyramidal symptoms and tardive dyskinesia because of their weaker D2 receptor antagonism compared to high-potency typicals.\

questions.reviewPearl: Lipophilic beta-blockers, such as propranolol, easily cross the blood-brain barrier and are notoriously associated with medication-induced depression, fatigue, and lethargy.\
questions.stem: A 47-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 4-week history of depressed mood, lack of energy, poor concentration, and daytime sleepiness. He started a weight loss program 6 weeks ago consisting of diet and exercise, and he has had a 5.9-kg (13-lb) weight loss during this period. He has hypertension and was switched from hydrochlorothiazide to propranolol at his last visit 8 weeks ago. He has no history of psychiatric illness. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and now weighs 97 kg (215 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m². His blood pressure is 135/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Mental status examination shows depressed mood and poor concentration. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Mood disorder due to a general medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Identify beta-blockers as a common pharmacological cause of medication-induced depression.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient developed acute symptoms of depression and lethargy shortly after starting a lipophilic beta-blocker (propranolol) 8 weeks ago. This temporal relationship identifies it as a substance/medication-induced mood disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Major depressive disorder is diagnosed only when a medical condition or medication side effect is ruled out as the primary etiology. Dysthymic disorder requires chronicity (at least 2 years).

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Lipophilic beta-blockers, such as propranolol, easily cross the blood-brain barrier and are notoriously associated with medication-induced depression, fatigue, and lethargy.\

questions.reviewPearl: Separation anxiety frequently presents with somatic complaints (stomachaches, headaches) specifically timed on school mornings as an avoidance mechanism to stay with the primary caregiver.\
questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because of difficulty at school since beginning first grade 6 weeks ago. His teachers state that he has been daydreaming frequently, does not complete his work, and does not participate in classroom discussions. His mother reports that he is very shy, will not go to sleepovers, and has stomachaches every weekday morning. He played on a soccer team, but he quit when his mother could not drive him to games and he had to carpool with another family. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, the boy sits on his mother's lap, does not make eye contact with the physician, and is hesitant to answer questions but seems eager to please. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose separation anxiety disorder utilizing characteristic behavioral and somatic presentations.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The child’s distress surrounding being away from his mother (e.g., refusing to carpool, refusing sleepovers) combined with somatic symptoms (stomachaches on school mornings) are hallmarks of separation anxiety disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: The "daydreaming" and lack of completion of work are secondary to his severe anxiety regarding separation from his mother, making ADHD an incorrect primary diagnosis. It is not age-appropriate behavior at 7 years old to exhibit this level of functional impairment.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Separation anxiety frequently presents with somatic complaints (stomachaches, headaches) specifically timed on school mornings as an avoidance mechanism to stay with the primary caregiver.\

questions.reviewPearl:
High insulin levels coupled with low/normal C-peptide in a non-diabetic patient with profound hypoglycemia is pathognomonic for exogenous (surreptitious) insulin administration.\
Here are the extracted questions, answer choices, and generated explanations for Questions 31 through 40 based on the source material.\
questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department immediately after being found comatose at her desk. During transport, a fingerstick blood glucose concentration is 15 mg/dL; after administration of 50% dextrose in water, she promptly becomes alert. She takes no medications. Her mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Examination shows no abnormalities. Her serum insulin concentration is increased, and serum C-peptide concentration is within normal limits. Serum concentrations of electrolytes, thyroxine (T4), and cortisol are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Surreptitious administration of an oral hypoglycemic agent

questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate the etiology of hypoglycemia using insulin and C-peptide assays.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body:
The patient exhibits severe hypoglycemia with an elevated insulin level but a 
normal
 C-peptide level. Exogenous insulin does not contain C-peptide, confirming she surreptitiously administered her mother’s insulin.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body:
An insulinoma or the abuse of sulfonylureas (oral hypoglycemics) triggers endogenous insulin release, which would cause an elevation of 
both
 insulin and C-peptide.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
High insulin levels coupled with low/normal C-peptide in a non-diabetic patient with profound hypoglycemia is pathognomonic for exogenous (surreptitious) insulin administration.\
Here are the extracted questions, answer choices, and generated explanations for Questions 31 through 40 based on the source material.\

questions.retrievalTag: Parkinson disease major depressive disorder weight loss
questions.reviewPearl: Depression affects up to 50% of Parkinson disease patients and can severely exacerbate both their cognitive decline and motor symptoms.\
questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with Parkinson disease is brought to the physician by his wife for a routine follow-up examination. His wife says that he has become increasingly withdrawn and tearful during the past 3 weeks. He has had poor energy and decreased sleep for 2 months. Current medications include carbidopa-levodopa and pramipexole. He has had a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss since his last visit 3 months ago. He is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and now weighs 41 kg (90 lb); BMI is 16 kg/m². His temperature is 38°C (100.4°F), pulse is 62/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows progression of the resting tremor of the hands and bradykinesia since his last visit. On mental status examination, he has a flat affect and downcast gaze. His speech is slow and soft. When asked about his crying, he says, "Who wouldn't be sad with this damn disease I've got?" He does not want to go anywhere because he does not want people to see him with his tremor. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's findings?

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Normal reaction to a chronic medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize major depressive disorder in the setting of chronic, neurodegenerative illnesses like Parkinson disease.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient’s persistent low mood, tearfulness, and profound neurovegetative symptoms (severe 15-lb weight loss, insomnia, and low energy) point to a clinical diagnosis of major depressive disorder, which is highly comorbid with Parkinson disease.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: A normal reaction or an adjustment disorder would not present with such severe, rapid weight loss and pervasive functional decline. Dysthymic disorder requires symptoms lasting for at least 2 years.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Depression affects up to 50% of Parkinson disease patients and can severely exacerbate both their cognitive decline and motor symptoms.\

questions.reviewPearl: Intramuscular formulations of second-generation antipsychotics (e.g., ziprasidone, olanzapine) are standard-of-care for rapidly calming severely agitated psychotic patients in the emergency department.\
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by police because she had been running in front of cars at a busy downtown intersection. She told police that she is on a mission to save the earth from pollution. On arrival, she is agitated and gestures animatedly as she swings her arms at staff in a threatening manner. She tells the staff that she needs to return to the street "to stop the internal combustion madness that's choking us all with smoke." Her medical history is unknown; she says she does not take any medications. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, her speech is increasingly loud and fast. She reports that she hears the voice of Mother Earth asking for her protection. The most appropriate initial step in pharmacotherapy for this patient's acute symptoms is administration of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Select an appropriate rapid-acting atypical antipsychotic for acute agitation and psychosis.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is presenting with acute agitation and active psychosis (delusions and auditory hallucinations) endangering herself and others. A fast-acting atypical antipsychotic like ziprasidone is the optimal initial pharmacotherapy for rapid chemical restraint.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Lithium and lamotrigine are mood stabilizers that take weeks to become effective and do not provide acute tranquilization. Fluoxetine and hydroxyzine do not treat acute psychosis.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Intramuscular formulations of second-generation antipsychotics (e.g., ziprasidone, olanzapine) are standard-of-care for rapidly calming severely agitated psychotic patients in the emergency department.\

questions.retrievalTag: Major depressive disorder SSRI escitalopram resident
questions.reviewPearl: Major depressive disorder in highly functioning individuals can initially present with prominent cognitive deficits ("pseudodementia") and severe anxiety before overt depressed mood is identified.\
questions.stem: A 27-year-old first-year surgical resident comes to the physician to request a prescription for a sleeping aid. He says that during the past 2 months, he has had increasing anxiety. On days when he is not on call, he spends hours reading medical texts because he cannot remember as well as he did in medical school. He avoids participating in social activities with friends. His wife has told him that he has been increasingly irritable. He falls asleep easily in the evening but wakes up multiple times during the night with the feeling that he has not read enough. He cannot sleep any later than 3 am even though he is tired. He and his wife now sleep in different bedrooms, so that she is not disturbed when he wakes up. He has had a 2.7-kg (6-lb) weight loss during this time. He recalls that he was always cheerful and reliable in medical school and did not experience this much anxiety. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he appears concerned and has a reactive affect. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassure the patient that this is a normal reaction to the first year of a residency program

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Advise the patient to consider alternatives to his surgical residency program

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Recommend over-the-counter diphenhydramine therapy as needed

questions.educationalObjective: Manage new-onset major depressive disorder presenting with prominent anxiety and cognitive complaints.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is exhibiting clear signs of a major depressive episode, including early morning awakenings, impaired concentration, anhedonia, weight loss, and severe anxiety. Escitalopram, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), is a first-line treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Reassurance is inappropriate because the patient is functionally impaired and losing weight. Quetiapine is an antipsychotic not typically used as first-line monotherapy for unipolar depression, and diphenhydramine will not treat the underlying mood disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Major depressive disorder in highly functioning individuals can initially present with prominent cognitive deficits ("pseudodementia") and severe anxiety before overt depressed mood is identified.\

questions.retrievalTag: Complex partial seizure temporal lobe focal spikes
questions.reviewPearl: Uncinate fits (hallucinations of unpleasant odors, such as burning rubber) are highly specific localizing auras for epileptiform activity in the mesial temporal lobe.\
questions.stem: A 42-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of a 2-month history of staring spells that last 1 to 2 minutes each. During episodes, he also smacks his lips and picks at his shirt collar. Four years ago, he was comatose for 2 weeks after sustaining a head injury in a motorcycle collision; he required 6 months of rehabilitation. He reports that over the past year, he has had intermittent episodes of smelling burnt rubber; episodes occur approximately every 2 weeks. He also hears an intense hissing sound during these episodes. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely finding on EEG?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Diffuse 3-Hz spike and slow wave activity

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Focal spikes localized to the temporal lobe

questions.educationalObjective: Localize complex partial seizures to the temporal lobe based on characteristic clinical auras and automatisms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing complex partial seizures (staring spells with lip-smacking automatisms) preceded by an olfactory aura (burnt rubber smell). This localizes the seizure focus strictly to the temporal lobe, revealing focal spikes on an EEG.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Diffuse 3-Hz spike-and-wave activity is characteristic of absence seizures, which lack complex sensory auras and typically affect children. Triphasic waves indicate metabolic encephalopathy.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Uncinate fits (hallucinations of unpleasant odors, such as burning rubber) are highly specific localizing auras for epileptiform activity in the mesial temporal lobe.\

questions.retrievalTag: Delusional disorder isolated delusions intact memory
questions.reviewPearl: Patients with delusional disorder often maintain an outwardly normal appearance and baseline functioning, aside from behaviors directly related to their core delusional beliefs.\
questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her parents because of strange behavior for 3 months. The parents say that she has insisted that her father and the next-door neighbor have been taking money away from her. She also says that fumes that her neighbors are blowing under her door are giving her headaches and changing her skin tone. On numerous occasions, she has called radio stations and newspapers about these events. She insists that she is healthy and is acting normally. She has no previous history of psychiatric disorders. Mental status examination shows no incoherence, loose associations, or hallucinations. She is oriented to person, place, and time, and her memory is intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose delusional disorder by distinguishing it from schizophrenia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient exhibits fixed delusions for more than 1 month but lacks other characteristic signs of schizophrenia (e.g., prominent hallucinations, disorganized speech, flat affect), and her cognitive function remains otherwise intact.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Schizophrenia and schizophreniform disorder require the presence of two or more psychotic symptoms (e.g., delusions plus hallucinations or disorganization) and typically feature a severe decline in functioning.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Patients with delusional disorder often maintain an outwardly normal appearance and baseline functioning, aside from behaviors directly related to their core delusional beliefs.\

questions.reviewPearl: When treating medication-induced psychosis in Parkinson disease, the initial step is always to reduce the anti-Parkinsonian medications carefully (starting with anticholinergics or dopamine agonists, then levodopa) before considering low-potency atypical antipsychotics like quetiapine or pimavanserin.\
questions.stem: An 87-year-old man who resides in a skilled nursing care facility is brought to the physician because of increased anxiety for 2 weeks. He says he feels a deep sense of foreboding that something terrible might happen but that he cannot elaborate as to what that might be. The staff nurses report that during the past month he has accused several of them of taking his things. He also has been heard talking when alone in his room, and when questioned about this, he referred to unseen visitors. He has a history of major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, and Parkinson disease. His current medications include venlafaxine and carbidopa-levodopa. One month ago, the venlafaxine dosage was doubled, and the combination carbidopa-levodopa was increased by 50%. Physical examination shows tremulousness. He has a shuffling gait. On mental status examination, he is alert and cooperative. He has a full range of affect. He worries that the staff is stealing from him, but he reports no hallucinations. He is oriented to person, place, and day but not to the date; he is off by 1 day. He recalls two of three words after 5 minutes and recalls the third with prompting. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Manage Parkinson disease medication-induced psychosis.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has developed medication-induced psychosis (paranoia, responding to unseen stimuli) directly following a recent 50% increase in his dopaminergic medication. The standard initial step is to decrease the dose of the offending agent.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Adding risperidone risks severely worsening his underlying Parkinsonian motor symptoms due to D2 antagonism. Discontinuing carbidopa-levodopa abruptly can cause akinetic crisis or neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: When treating medication-induced psychosis in Parkinson disease, the initial step is always to reduce the anti-Parkinsonian medications carefully (starting with anticholinergics or dopamine agonists, then levodopa) before considering low-potency atypical antipsychotics like quetiapine or pimavanserin.\

questions.reviewPearl: SSRIs are generally first-line for OCD, but clomipramine is the most specific and heavily utilized tricyclic antidepressant for treating both initial and refractory OCD.\
questions.stem: A 21-year-old college student comes to the physician because of a 3-year history of distressing thoughts that he will hurt other people. He counts objects to prevent these thoughts from happening. These thoughts have become more frequent over the past 2 months, and he can no longer concentrate on his studies or sleep at night. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he states that he has violent sexual thoughts that he cannot control. He is oriented to person, place, and time, and his memory is intact. He says that he is embarrassed talking about these thoughts. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Choose an effective pharmacological treatment for obsessive-compulsive disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is distressed by ego-dystonic obsessions (intrusive violent/sexual thoughts) and employs compulsions (counting), confirming obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). Clomipramine is a highly serotonergic tricyclic antidepressant that is highly effective for OCD.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Haloperidol and diazepam are not primary treatments for OCD. While amitriptyline is also a TCA, clomipramine possesses significantly more potent serotonin-reuptake inhibition making it uniquely indicated for OCD.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: SSRIs are generally first-line for OCD, but clomipramine is the most specific and heavily utilized tricyclic antidepressant for treating both initial and refractory OCD.\

questions.reviewPearl: Conversion disorder frequently emerges as a subconscious defense mechanism where severe emotional distress is "converted" into a disabling physical neurological symptom.\
questions.stem: A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of progressive loss of voice since her husband of 20 years died 10 months ago. She communicates with the physician in writing and reports that she initially assumed her hoarse voice was a result of her prolonged, loud crying, but the hoarseness has become increasingly more severe even though she no longer cries as much. During the past 2 months, her voice has been so weak that she has had to communicate by writing. Since her husband died, she has felt very sad and cries herself to sleep nightly. She has difficulty falling asleep initially but then sleeps through the night. She notes that it was her fault her husband died. On the day he died, they had argued because she had just found out he was having an affair. During their argument, she told him that she wished he would die. A few hours later, police informed her that he had died in a motor vehicle collision. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has a blunted affect but becomes tearful and tense when addressing the argument with her husband. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient’s isolated aphonia (neurological motor symptom) triggered acutely by extreme psychological conflict/stress (wishing her husband dead right before his fatal crash) with no underlying organic pathology is characteristic of conversion disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While the patient is grieving, Major Depressive Disorder does not explain her physical loss of voice. Adjustment disorder is too broad and fails to account for the specific somatoform presentation.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Conversion disorder frequently emerges as a subconscious defense mechanism where severe emotional distress is "converted" into a disabling physical neurological symptom.\

questions.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines form the absolute cornerstone of treating alcohol withdrawal; providers must be willing to titrate doses to very high levels to achieve symptom control, especially in mechanically ventilated patients.\
questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 12-hour history of tremors and confusion. She has a history of alcoholism and last consumed alcohol 24 hours ago. She has no other history of serious illness. She does not smoke cigarettes or use illicit drugs. On admission, she is agitated, diaphoretic, and oriented to person but not to time or place. Vitamin B1 (thiamine), folic acid, and three doses of diazepam are administered intravenously. Thirty-five minutes later, respiratory compromise develops; the patient is intubated, and mechanical ventilation is started at a respiratory rate of 20/min. Two hours later, she remains agitated. She is pulling at her peripheral intravenous and urethral catheters and picking at her blankets. Her pulse is 140/min, and blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg.
