Neurologic examination shows tremor of the upper and lower extremities and hyperreflexia bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Add flumazenil to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Add haloperidol to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Add phenytoin to the medication regimen

questions.educationalObjective: Manage severe, refractory alcohol withdrawal using aggressive benzodiazepine dosing when the patient's airway is secure.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is enduring severe alcohol withdrawal (delirium tremens), causing life-threatening autonomic instability. Because her airway is secured via intubation, the priority is to aggressively administer additional diazepam to regain control of the autonomic hyperactivity and prevent seizures.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Flumazenil (a benzodiazepine antagonist) would instantly precipitate seizures. Haloperidol can lower the seizure threshold, and phenytoin is ineffective for alcohol withdrawal seizures.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Benzodiazepines form the absolute cornerstone of treating alcohol withdrawal; providers must be willing to titrate doses to very high levels to achieve symptom control, especially in mechanically ventilated patients.\

questions.reviewPearl:
The diagnostic threshold for primary amenorrhea is no menses by age 13 
without
 secondary sexual characteristics, or no menses by age 15 
with
 normal secondary sexual characteristics.\

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because she has not yet had a menstrual period. Her mother states that the patient's older sister began menstruating at the age of 11 years. The patient has been treated for major depressive disorder with fluoxetine for the past 8 months. She is not sexually active. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Sexual development is Tanner stage 2. Mental status examination shows mild depression and anxiety. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Referral for reassessment by her psychiatrist

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize normal physiological timelines for female puberty to prevent unnecessary diagnostic testing.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is 13 years old and exhibits signs of breast development (Tanner stage 2). Primary amenorrhea is not diagnosed until age 15 if secondary sexual characteristics are present, making her lack of menses entirely normal for her age. Reassurance and discussion are indicated.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Fluoxetine does not cause amenorrhea. Serum prolactin evaluation is unwarranted since the patient is currently within the standard physiological timeline for pubertal development.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
The diagnostic threshold for primary amenorrhea is no menses by age 13 
without
 secondary sexual characteristics, or no menses by age 15 
with
 normal secondary sexual characteristics.\

questions.retrievalTag: Acute severe pain history of opioid dependence morphine
questions.reviewPearl: A history of substance abuse is not a contraindication to adequate opioid analgesia for acute, severe trauma.\
questions.stem: A 47-year-old man is admitted to the hospital 20 minutes after he was involved in a motor vehicle collision. He was an unrestrained passenger. He has a history of cocaine abuse and meperidine dependence but has not used illicit drugs for 9 years. He takes no medications. Physical examination shows several deep lacerations on the lower extremities. There are no other injuries. Serum and urine toxicology screening is negative for opioids, cocaine, and alcohol. Extensive wound debridement and suturing are planned. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management of this patient's pain?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Acupuncture and application of cold packs

questions.educationalObjective: Manage acute severe pain appropriately with opioids even in patients with a remote history of substance use disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has acute, severe nociceptive pain requiring immediate surgical debridement; a remote history of substance use disorder does not preclude the use of appropriate, fully dosed opioid analgesia (morphine) for acute severe pain.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Non-opioid options like ibuprofen or physical modalities are insufficient for severe acute traumatic pain. Gabapentin/amitriptyline are meant for chronic neuropathic pain.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: A history of substance abuse is not a contraindication to adequate opioid analgesia for acute, severe trauma.\

questions.retrievalTag: Bipolar disorder lithium discontinuation risk of recurrence
questions.reviewPearl: Discontinuing lithium maintenance therapy in bipolar patients, particularly abruptly, carries a relapse rate of up to 50% within the first few months.\
questions.stem: A 23-year-old man with a 3-year history of bipolar disorder well controlled with lithium carbonate would like to discontinue drug therapy because of adverse effects. He is upset because he has gained 6.8 kg (15 lb) and developed acne. He has tried trials of other mood stabilizers that were ineffective in controlling his symptoms. He has a 2-year history of social isolation characterized by fears that he will be rejected by women because of his weight gain and acne. He recently graduated and is concerned about his ability to get a job because of mandatory drug testing. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Write a letter about lithium carbonate therapy for the patient to give to his employer

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Explain the risk of illness recurrence without medication

questions.educationalObjective: Counsel patients with bipolar disorder on the high risk of relapse upon discontinuation of maintenance mood stabilizers.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient wishes to stop a highly effective medication for bipolar disorder. The immediate physician responsibility is to ensure informed consent by explaining the high risk of manic/depressive relapse (illness recurrence) if lithium is discontinued.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Changing therapy is inappropriate because he has failed other mood stabilizers, and fluoxetine could trigger mania. Writing a letter does not address the primary medical risk of stopping therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Discontinuing lithium maintenance therapy in bipolar patients, particularly abruptly, carries a relapse rate of up to 50% within the first few months.\

questions.retrievalTag: Acute dystonia torticollis haloperidol dopamine antagonism
questions.reviewPearl: Acute dystonia is one of the earliest extrapyramidal side effects to appear (hours to days) after initiating D2 receptor antagonists and is treated with anticholinergics like benztropine or diphenhydramine.\
questions.stem: Fifteen minutes after administration of intramuscular haloperidol, a 21-year-old woman who is in the hospital has torticollis. She has a 1-month history of psychotic disorder and was admitted to the hospital 4 hours ago because of the acute onset of new delusions and agitated behavior. She appears frightened. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows lateral rotation of the head to the right with a right sternocleidomastoid muscle that is taut to palpation. Antagonism of which of the following neurotransmitters is most likely responsible for this patient's condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify dopamine antagonism as the mechanism underlying acute dystonia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing an acute dystonic reaction (torticollis) caused by high-potency typical antipsychotic use (haloperidol), which functions via potent antagonism of dopamine D2 receptors in the nigrostriatal pathway.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Antagonism of acetylcholine, histamine, or norepinephrine causes anticholinergic, sedating, or hypotensive side effects, respectively, not extrapyramidal motor symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Acute dystonia is one of the earliest extrapyramidal side effects to appear (hours to days) after initiating D2 receptor antagonists and is treated with anticholinergics like benztropine or diphenhydramine.\

questions.retrievalTag: Generalized anxiety disorder chronic worry muscle tension
questions.reviewPearl: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for at least 6 months, often presenting with physical symptoms like muscle tension and sleep disturbance.\
questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-year history of intermittent headaches and diarrhea that have become more frequent and intense during the past month. She reports that after college graduation 3 months ago, her boyfriend of 3 years ended their relationship and left to take a job in another city. Two weeks ago, she started working in an entry-level accounting position for a local company. She is worried about how she will perform at her new job and whether she will have enough money to pay her bills and her school loans. She recalls that for much of her final year of college she was concerned about whether she would graduate and find a job. Her many thoughts often made it difficult to sleep and concentrate on her schoolwork. She states that she has always been a worrier and would frequently ask her boyfriend for reassurance that he would not leave her. She says that her friends describe her as often irritable and restless but bright. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 85/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 135/85 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for muscle tension in the neck and shoulder. On mental status examination, she appears restless and anxious. She says that her mood varies depending on what she is doing at the moment. Her thought process is organized, and there is no evidence of suicidal ideation or psychosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose generalized anxiety disorder based on chronic, excessive worry and associated somatic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient exhibits chronic (multi-year), pervasive, and uncontrollable worry about various domains (school, job, money, relationships) accompanied by somatic symptoms (muscle tension, headaches, diarrhea, sleep difficulties), which fits the criteria for generalized anxiety disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder requires symptoms occurring within 3 months of a stressor and resolving within 6 months, whereas her worry is chronic (years).

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for at least 6 months, often presenting with physical symptoms like muscle tension and sleep disturbance.\

questions.reviewPearl: Dissociative fugue typically follows severe psychological trauma or stress and is characterized by sudden, unexpected travel away from home with an inability to recall one's past or personal identity.\
questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by police after local shopkeepers complained that she had been begging in front of their stores for 4 days. She appears malnourished, dirty, and confused. She is friendly and cooperative but is unable to give her name. She says that she is hungry and had been begging for food and money. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for mild dehydration. On mental status examination, she is oriented to place and time but is unable to recall her own identity or where she lives. She has a full affect and a neutral mood. Psychomotor activity is normal. Her thoughts are goal directed. Her hematocrit is 45%, and serum sodium concentration is 145 mEq/L. An MRI of the brain shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose dissociative fugue based on identity loss and purposeful travel.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The sudden loss of autobiographical memory (unable to give her name or recall her identity) accompanied by purposeful travel/wandering (begging in a new location) and intact basic cognitive functioning is pathognomonic for dissociative fugue.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Transient global amnesia affects the ability to form new memories (anterograde amnesia) but personal identity is spared. Complex partial seizures involve altered consciousness and automatisms, not sustained identity loss.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Dissociative fugue typically follows severe psychological trauma or stress and is characterized by sudden, unexpected travel away from home with an inability to recall one's past or personal identity.\

questions.reviewPearl: Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant CAG trinucleotide repeat disorder that presents with a triad of chorea, cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms (often starting with personality changes).\
questions.stem: A 53-year-old woman is brought to the physician by her husband because of changes in personality over the past 6 years. Her husband says that she used to be easygoing and quiet but has become irritable, sexually uninhibited, and loud. She recently embarrassed him in public. She has also had intermittent jerky movements during this period. Her father had a history of similar symptoms at the age of 56 years. She takes no medications and does not drink alcohol or smoke. She appears unkempt and restless, is rude, and makes dirty jokes during the examination. Examination shows irregular, rapid, jerky movements of the tongue and lower extremities, and the torso extends in quick jerks. Complete blood count and serum concentrations of vitamin B12 (cobalamin), folic acid, and thyroid-stimulating hormone are within normal limits. Serologic testing for syphilis is negative. An MRI of the brain is most likely to show which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify the classic MRI finding associated with Huntington disease.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The clinical picture of progressive personality changes (disinhibition, irritability), choreiform movements (jerky, rapid movements), and a positive family history points directly to Huntington disease, which is characterized by atrophy of the caudate nucleus.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Frontal lobe atrophy characterizes frontotemporal dementia, which lacks chorea. Enlarged lateral ventricles are a nonspecific finding often seen in schizophrenia.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant CAG trinucleotide repeat disorder that presents with a triad of chorea, cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms (often starting with personality changes).\

questions.retrievalTag: Spiral fracture femur infant child abuse
questions.reviewPearl: Any fracture in a child who is not yet walking independently should mandate a thorough investigation for child abuse.\
questions.stem: A 10-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of irritability throughout the night. His mother says that he has been crying and has refused to bear weight on his lower extremities since she picked him up from the babysitter the previous afternoon. She is concerned that he may have hurt himself while in the babysitter's care. Examination shows tenderness to palpation of the right thigh. An x-ray shows mild demineralization of bone and a spiral fracture of the right femur. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of injury?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize spiral fractures in non-ambulatory infants as a red flag for child abuse.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: A spiral fracture of the femur in a non-ambulatory 10-month-old infant is highly suspicious for non-accidental trauma (child abuse), specifically caused by a forceful twisting motion.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While osteogenesis imperfecta causes frequent fractures, spiral fractures of long bones in infants strongly suggest abuse until proven otherwise.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Any fracture in a child who is not yet walking independently should mandate a thorough investigation for child abuse.\

questions.reviewPearl: Propranolol, a non-selective beta-blocker, is frequently used on an as-needed basis to manage the autonomic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia) of the performance subtype of social anxiety disorder.\
questions.stem: A 20-year-old college student comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of anxiety, dry mouth, sweating, nausea, and palpitations. She first noticed her symptoms when she tried to join a sorority and became embarrassed when she was asked to speak in front of the group. She says that she is failing a public speaking class because of overwhelming anxiety when she stands in front of the class to speak. She now avoids situations where she might have to speak in public. She says that her anxiety is "stupid" and thought taking a public speaking class would help with her symptoms. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose social phobia based on performance-related anxiety and avoidance.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient experiences severe, debilitating anxiety exclusively triggered by social performance situations (public speaking), leading to avoidance behavior. This is diagnostic of social anxiety disorder (social phobia), performance type.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Panic disorder involves unexpected, unprovoked panic attacks. Specific phobia is tied to a specific object or non-social situation (e.g., heights, spiders).

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Propranolol, a non-selective beta-blocker, is frequently used on an as-needed basis to manage the autonomic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia) of the performance subtype of social anxiety disorder.\

questions.reviewPearl: Naltrexone and disulfiram are generally contraindicated in patients with acute hepatitis or severe hepatic failure; psychosocial rehabilitation remains the primary intervention.\
questions.stem: A 37-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination 3 months after being diagnosed with alcoholic cirrhosis. He has missed three appointments that he attributes to transportation problems. His driver's license was suspended 3 months ago for driving under the influence of alcohol. He does not believe that his drinking is a problem, but he has cut down the amount he drinks from 12 beers daily to 4 beers daily. He is disheveled and restless and claims that he has not slept well for the past 3 months. His pulse is 96/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 170/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities except for peripheral edema of the lower extremities. On mental status examination, he describes his mood as fine, but he is irritable and has poor eye contact, especially when discussing his drinking. He speaks at a normal rate and with normal response latency. His thought process is logical and coherent without evidence of psychosis. His serum albumin concentration is 3 g/dL, total serum bilirubin concentration is 2.6 mg/dL, and serum -glutamyltransferase (GGT) activity is 158 U/L (N=5–50). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Manage alcohol use disorder in the setting of severe hepatic dysfunction.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has decompensated alcoholic cirrhosis and an active, severe alcohol use disorder with poor insight. The most definitive and appropriate management is referring him to a comprehensive alcohol rehabilitation program to achieve total abstinence.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Disulfiram is contraindicated in severe liver disease. Liver transplant evaluation requires at least 6 months of documented abstinence. Diazepam is not indicated acutely as he is not in severe acute withdrawal.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Naltrexone and disulfiram are generally contraindicated in patients with acute hepatitis or severe hepatic failure; psychosocial rehabilitation remains the primary intervention.\

questions.retrievalTag: Brief psychotic disorder acute onset resolution under 1 month
questions.reviewPearl: Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by psychotic symptoms lasting at least 1 day but less than 1 month, followed by a full return to the premorbid level of functioning, frequently triggered by intense emotional stress.\
questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of auditory hallucinations during the past week. She says that she was the lead actress in a major new play that received negative reviews and closed after one performance 8 days ago. She was shocked when she heard the news and retreated to her country house where she closed all the curtains and disconnected the phones and computers. The next day, she heard a voice intermittently making derogatory comments about her career. She refused to answer the door when dry cleaning was delivered because she believed that the delivery man was a journalist in disguise. A few days later, she fell asleep in the evening and had a full night's rest for the first time since the play's opening night. She says that when she woke up, she no longer heard the voice. She has a history of alcoholism and has been hospitalized twice for detoxification, but she has abstained from alcohol for the past year. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, her thoughts are logical and goal directed. She says that she no longer is having any auditory or visual hallucinations. She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose brief psychotic disorder based on symptom duration and sudden onset.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient experienced acute onset of psychosis (auditory hallucinations, paranoia) following a severe psychological stressor (play closing), with symptoms lasting less than 1 month and fully resolving, which strictly defines brief psychotic disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Acute stress disorder is related to life-threatening trauma. Schizophrenia requires symptoms lasting over 6 months. Substance-induced psychosis is unlikely given her 1-year abstinence from alcohol.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by psychotic symptoms lasting at least 1 day but less than 1 month, followed by a full return to the premorbid level of functioning, frequently triggered by intense emotional stress.\
